posts.name: Bill Moore
posts.cooked:
Of all the Bordeaux communes, Graves/Pessac is dearest to my heart. It was Graves wines that got me into BDX in the first place, and their savory, smoky, and earthy appeal has kept me coming back. And within Graves, it’s Haut Brion, Pape Clement, and Haut Bailly that stand above the others in my book. Backfilling them has been great: Pape Clement’s run from 1985-1990 was especially tremendous, and the wines from that stretch are really worth seeking out. However, with Haut Brion’s pricing now in the stratosphere and Pape Clement’s long-lamented decline into Spoofdom, that leaves only Haut Bailly as an option for more recent vintages.

That said, I’ve never had any Haut Bailly more recent than the (most excellent) 2001 vintage. Since then, I know they’ve made investments and renovations at the property and that those changes have coincided with both higher Parker scores and middling Gilman scores, both of which are worrying trends for me. The wine is probably the only BDX for which “finesse” is my reflexive descriptor, and I’d hate to see its earthy elegance disappear under a cloak of oak and layers of blueberry, mocha, etc.

Has anyone who’s had recent Haut Bailly vintages noticed a change in style? If so, how do you feel it’s changed?

Cheers,

Bill

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Curious as well.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I have quite a bit of experience with these wines in both their older and more recent incarnations, and would say that you can buy Haut Bailly with confidence.

Their vintages 2010 and 2009 were certainly a bit denser and riper than is the norm, but that is true pretty much across the board in all Bordeaux, including addresses which were not looking to push the boundaries, such as Montrose or VCC. New oak percentages range between 50% and 65%, which is pretty typical these days—a similar or even a bit lower than the numbers you find at Montrose and VCC to stick with those examples. A percentage of the production goes through malo in barrel, for what that’s worth, though that’s a complex subject. And Veronique Sanders, who I admire very much, has a classical palate and sensibility. A few years ago she presented a tasting from my former tasting group in Oxford, and we were very much on the same page in our appreciation of the magnum of 1970 Pape Clement we all enjoyed over dinner afterwards, which was pretty far from being a modernist Graves!

If you’re looking for classically-proportioned vintages, the 1998 is a very undervalued maturing example (probably a decade from really expatiating), and one of Veronique’s favorites. The 2006 is very creditable and also a bit overlooked. And the 2008 is just terrific, with lots of tension and vibrancy. 2012 is very nice too, but cut from more open-knit, supple cloth, and likely to give pleasure just a bit earlier than the very concentrated, long-haul 2008.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
Good questions, plus it’s also one of the Bordeaux houses that I generally prefer.

The last, i.e. most recent, vintage that I’ve had was the 2005 and that was about 7 years ago when it was just released. We usually have a Bordeaux vintage “let’s try ‘em out” offline at every release when MattR used to get it off the ground for our local Bdx group. While it has the 2005 vin-de-garde stamp all over it, I thought then that it was still true to its elegant, traditional house style and definitely did not go the Pape Clement route. A few days later, I followed up our tasting with a 6-pack purchase for long term safe-keeping.

A 1995 version during a Bordeaux horizontal dinner last year, was double decanted earlier in the day, was elegant, and generally pleased the majority of the diners, and provided us with a nice classic example of a Graves wine.

The 1999 version is a classical beauty, imho.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: They are richer and denser no doubt but not in the spoof zone. I don’t think they’re any better than when they were scoring 88-91, but they’re a lot more expensive.

posts.name: Bill Moore

William,

This is great info, thanks. It’s reassuring to hear that they’ve retained their special character. I will definitely look into the recent, less-celebrated vintages you cited.

Nice also to hear about the 1970 P-C! The 1970 Haut Bailly is a benchmark BDX for me and was my WOTY for 2014.

Thanks again,

Bill

posts.name: Bill Moore
posts.cooked:
Keith Levenberg:

They are richer and denser no doubt but not in the spoof zone. I don’t think they’re any better than when they were scoring 88-91, but they’re a lot more expensive.

I found the price hike on the 2010 especially galling (and part of the reason I keep buying older vintages instead). For those who doubt Parker’s continued impact on pricing, look no further than the 2010 Haut Bailly.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Keith Levenberg:

They are richer and denser no doubt but not in the spoof zone. I don’t think they’re any better than when they were scoring 88-91, but they’re a lot more expensive.

I commend Keith for being able to distinguish between riper/richer/denser and spoofy. People often assume the former means the latter, but it doesn’t necessarily.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:

This is great info, thanks. It’s reassuring to hear that they’ve retained their special character. I will definitely look into the recent, less-celebrated vintages you cited.

My pleasure Bill.

The 1970 Pape Clement is a terrific wine, but quite high in acidity for Pape Clem. It really pops after three hours in the decanter.

The best surprise I ever had with Pape Clement was the '52, a generally despised vintage. But the '52 PC could pass for a '55 or even a '53. I remember opening the first bottle rather casually with low expectations, then having to call up everyone interested in wine in the neighborhood so they could have a taste, as it was too good not to share! That ended up being one of the most pleasure-giving cases of wine I ever purchased. I speculate other '52 Graves might be interesting too, but have never encountered any.

All best,
William.

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked: FWIW, I consider myself to have a traditional plate like those posting in this thread and I found a 96 Pape Clement last year to be very appealing. The fruit is round and plush to be sure, but there was plenty of earthy rusticity as well. I believe those on here that the newer examples are spoofed, but maybe it started later than some think…?
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.cooked: I have had many vintages of Pape Clement from 1996 to present and I have never found it to be anything other a classic well balanced example of Bordeaux. The 2010 is practically perfect and not the slightest hint of spoof.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: The overt spoof at Pape Clement began around 1998 and got even more cartoonish in subsequent years.

posts.name: Carlos Delpin
posts.cooked: The 2001 Pape Clement in my experience is a classic Bordeaux at all levels. Not an ounce of spoof in that wine.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Carlos, Bob:

Try the 2005 Pape Clement. It’s the classic example of a wine that was pumped up in the cellar and then deflated in bottle; the journey from rich and textural to obviously oaky, spirituous and diffuse took only ten years.

Since circa 2000 Pape Clement has become an entirely different wine, but it’s a great property.

Best,
William

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked:
Carlos Delpin:

The 2001 Pape Clement in my experience is a classic Bordeaux at all levels. Not an ounce of spoof in that wine.

Will have to agree to disagree on that one. Only thing I can say is to consider tasting it next to the 2001 Haut-Bailly. The difference will be dramatic.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
Craig G:

For all the hand-wringing about Pape Clement’s descent into the abyss, it’s pretty difficult to find a poor tasting note on those wines from 1998 onwards.

That’s because those of us who hate what it’s become stopped buying after 1998.

The 1998 was a bit of a mid-way point (I like it very much but it certainly moved in a very modern direction). Never tried the the 1999 but the 2000 was a caricature of what it PC used to be. For people who like the more Rollandish wines it’s a buy but it’s definitely not so for me.

I don’t think I tried '99 or '00 near release (have since tried '00 and not liked). When '01 futures were out Parker’s notes said something like best PC ever. As a PC fan ('90 PC ranks with '86/'89 Meyneys, '79 du Tertre, '78 DDC as probably only Bdx I’ve bought more than a case of) I thought a must have, and bought a case. Not a happy camper when I tried. I’ve drank several (I’ll admit last bottle couple years ago was at least better than 10+ years ago) but given as gifts to friends with more modern tastes or used in lots I’ve donated to charity auctions. Happy to be down to 1 bottle.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked: I can recall a GJE blind tasting in Chamonix over 3 days with top critics such as Michelle Bettane when all the important Bordeaux of vintage 2001 were on the table. Among the top 10 from memory: Lafite, Petrus, Ausone … and Pape Clement! I was a guest taster and it was at the top of my list as well. The best wine was for me: Petrus. But the Pape Clement was´t that far behind. Top wine IMO.

I own a bottle of the 2005 but have not had it, so I cannot comment. I can say that when I tasted the 2010 I immediately thought this was classic old school Bordeaux. More structured and delineated than the 1990 and 1996 which are 2 favorites of mine and would eclipse those wines easily.

I would be curious if anyone who thought that they were modernist or spoofy have tried the 2010 and what they thought

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked:
Here are some recent Pape Clement notes:

  • 2013 Château Pape Clément - France, Bordeaux, Graves, Pessac-Léognan (1/27/2016)
    UGC 2016 (Chicago, IL): More barrel than grape, one wonders if the grapes were an inadvertent addition. (70 pts.)
  • 2012 Château Pape Clément - France, Bordeaux, Graves, Pessac-Léognan (1/28/2015)
    UGC 2015 (Chicago, IL): Would you like some wine with your oak? (70 pts.)

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked: Well palates are different. Do you think the oak integrates when the wine is at peak in 15 years? This happens quite often.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Jürgen Steinke:

Well palates are different. Do you think the oak integrates when the wine is at peak in 15 years? This happens quite often.

On a related thread, I posted the following:

I do not know whether this statement is correct, but for me, it is sorta beside the point. I do not like higher alcohol wines, riper wines and wines with lavish use of oak. What I have found over my years of wine appreciation is that (1) I tend to have a preference for mature reds that show ancillary and tertiary characteristics and (2) after 15+ years, I do not see alcohol, and quite often oak, resolving, and thus they tend to remain pronounced and mask or interfere with the perception of the characteristics that I value in a mature wine. I know I hear often that oak resolves, but I do not generally find that to be true. A perfect recent example for me was the 1995 Pahlmeyer Proprietary Red. It was hot.

Some recent Bordeaux that I have abhorred because of, inter alia, the lavish use of oak: 2000 Ch. Troplong Mondot, 2001 Barde-Haut. And while not in the “hate” category, I really did not like the 2003 Leoville Poyferre. And within your 15-year window, I really did not like recent examples of 2005 Pape Clement, 2005 Lascombe and a whole host of 2005 St. Ems that I cleared entirely from my cellar. Many of these St ems were blocks of wood, some still expressing the bitter astringency of new oak.

I had some well-known Cabs this past Monday, including the new 2013 release of Scarecrow M. Etain, which seems to garner high praise, and all I noticed was bitter astringency. I did not know what this wine was at the time, but my immediate thought, beyond that I hated it, was that this oak will never resolve.

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked:
Marc Frontario:

2005 was plonk on release, maybe better now…I assure u this is the exception not the rule

A recent note from my 00/05 comparative tasting:

  • 2005 Château Smith Haut Lafitte - France, Bordeaux, Graves, Pessac-Léognan (10/29/2015)
    Annual Comparative Bordeaux Tasting (2000/2005) (Chicago, IL): What’s with people putting more oak in the 2005? Reasonably elegant, but this is going down that “shapeless mass” path that I noticed in the 2000. Sweeter, riper fruit than the 2000, though the slightly rotten, overripe quality isn’t as prevalent here. Unfortunately though, the oak does make a much bigger (and unwelcome) entrance here, and a slight but further drop in acidity from the 2000 makes this the less desirable of the two. (85 pts.)

posts.name: Bill Moore
posts.cooked:
With my OP, I was more interested in the changes at Haut Bailly rather than revisiting the debate on spoofulation of recent Pape Clement, which is already well-trod ground: The spoofulation history of Ch. Pape Clement ?? - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers. And I’m not talking about relative ripeness here. The 1990 PC is plenty ripe, but it also screams Graves through and through. The 2009 PC I tasted (mercifully, only once) showed absolutely zero terroir, and I’m not optimistic it ever will. My fear was that Haut Bailly had made comparable style shifts, but it appears from those chiming in that it has not.

That said, anyone have an idea on why Gilman’s scores for recent Haut Bailly are so mediocre? He’s not flaming them a la Pavie (or Pape Clement), but he’s not really showing the love either. His is a palate I generally trust, so his so-so scores give me pause.

link_counts.title: The spoofulation history of Ch. Pape Clement ?? - Wine Berserkers - international wine social media, online community, and discussion

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: I’ve enjoyed this thread even with the drifts as they do relate. I’ve had my eye on the 2010 Haut Bailly, and have been waiting to see. Pricey at $145 or so, not sure if I interpret Keith to mean he does not think it is worth the fare.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Bill Moore:

That said, anyone have an idea on why Gilman’s scores for recent Haut Bailly are so mediocre? He’s not flaming them a la Pavie (or Pape Clement), but he’s not really showing the love either. His is a palate I generally trust, so his so-so scores give me pause.

Bill:

Another interesting question. Hopefully John will chime in. If I may speculate in the meantime, I’d venture that Haut Bailly’s comparatively slighter frame means that it shows its new wood a bit more obviously during its elevage than sturdier-structured wines such as Montrose that receive similar percentages of new cooperage.

From bottle the wines have not seemed unduly woody to me, even just after bottling, so those additional months in barrel after the EP tastings seem to do the trick—at least for my palate.

Best,
William.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
To me, there is no spoof in Haut Bailly with the possible exception of 2009, and I really should retaste it, as my notes here are inconsistent . 2010 was spoofless, and very, very classic. The 2008 was one of the top wines of the vintage, showing incredibly well at the Burdigala last year in NY.

Overall Veronique Sanders has done an extraordinary job over the years.

posts.name: Alan C h a n
posts.cooked: Haut Bailly is definitely keepin it real in my book. There was an amazing consistency through vintages that are very disparate in conditions. Sadly, they weren’t there this year and I fear it means they won’t take part anymore.
